To make the dough, start by warming the milk until it feels just slightly above body temperature, then whisk in the yeast and a tablespoon of the sugar and let it stand for about ten minutes until it looks foamy. In a large bowl, combine the flour, the remaining sugar, and the salt. Pour in the yeast mixture, the melted butter, and both eggs, and stir until a shaggy dough forms. Turn it out onto a floured counter and knead for eight to ten minutes. It should feel smooth and slightly tacky, but not sticky. Place it in an oiled bowl, cover it with a damp towel, and let it rise somewhere warm for about an hour, until it has doubled.

The strange thing about the lighthouse was not that it still worked, but that nobody in town could say who maintained it. Every evening at dusk the lamp would stutter twice and then settle into its slow, patient sweep across the bay, and every morning it went dark without ceremony. Children claimed an old keeper lived in the round room at the top, though no boat ever brought supplies and no light ever showed in the keeper's window. When the historical society finally forced the door one October, they found the stairs swept clean, a kettle still warm on the stove, and one hundred and forty years of logbooks, the last entry dated that very morning, written in a careful hand: weather fair, all lamps trimmed, nothing to report.
